data.title: Jaguar Mining (TSX:JAG) Quarterly Profit Of US$13 Million Tests Persistent Loss Narrative
data.description:
Jaguar Mining (TSX:JAG) has just posted its FY 2025 third quarter numbers, reporting revenue of US$34.0 million and EPS of US$0.16. The trailing twelve month figures show revenue of US$139.5 million and a loss of US$0.19 per share. Over recent periods the company’s quarterly revenue has moved between US$27.3 million and US$44.8 million, with EPS ranging from a loss of US$0.25 to a profit of US$0.40. This latest result therefore fits into a track record marked by sharp earnings volatility. For investors, a key consideration is how sustainable the current margin profile appears, given that the trailing twelve month line remains in loss territory.

See our full analysis for Jaguar Mining.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to compare these results with the prevailing narratives around growth, profitability and risk to see which still hold up and which may now be outdated.

See what the community is saying about Jaguar Mining

Advertisement

13.0 million net income swings TTM loss picture

  • Q3 FY 2025 net income of US$13.0 million contrasts with a trailing twelve month loss of US$15.1 million on US$139.5 million of revenue, highlighting how different a single profitable quarter can look compared with the full year picture.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that this positive quarter sits against five year annualised loss growth of 50.7%, which means anyone optimistic on a turnaround needs to weigh one strong period against a longer stretch of widening losses.
    • Supporters of the bullish case may point to Q3 profitability alongside analyst forecasts that earnings could grow around 10.94% per year, but the trailing twelve month loss shows the business is not there yet.
    • Critics of that bullish angle can highlight that net profit margins over the last year have not improved and remain negative on a trailing basis, so the recent profit needs to be tested over more than one quarter.

Revenue steady, profitability still catching up

  • Over the last six reported quarters, revenue per period stayed in a relatively tight band between US$27.3 million and US$44.8 million, while trailing twelve month revenue moved from US$158.6 million to US$139.5 million as the company remained loss making over that span.
  • Bears arguing that the business model is too fragile focus on this mix of stable revenue with trailing losses, and the data here gives them material points to work with.
    • On the risk side, the company is currently unprofitable over the last 12 months and losses have grown at 50.7% per year over five years, which matches the bearish concern about earnings pressure.
    • At the same time, forecasts calling for revenue growth of about 10.9% per year and an expected return to profitability within three years give a direct counterpoint to the idea that current loss levels are permanent.

Valuation gap between CA$6.93 price and DCF fair value

  • The shares trade at CA$6.93, while a DCF fair value of CA$30.24 implies the stock is about 77.1% below that estimate, and the current P/S of roughly 3x sits under both the 7.3x industry level and 16.2x peer average.
  • Consensus style thinking that sees this as a potential value situation has clear support from those valuation gaps, but it runs straight into the reality of current losses and margin pressure.
    • On the supportive side, the combination of a lower P/S multiple and a DCF fair value that is far above the market price is exactly what valuation focused investors often look for when revenue is forecast to grow around 10.9% per year.
    • On the cautionary side, the business is still loss making on a trailing twelve month basis and historical profit quality is weak, so the discount could also reflect the market’s focus on execution risk rather than a simple mispricing.

data.title: Alibaba’s AI Push Shifts From Open Source To Enterprise Monetization
data.description:
• Alibaba Group Holding (NYSE:BABA) has introduced new proprietary AI models, Qwen3.5-Omni and Qwen3.6-Plus, featuring multimodal and real time multilingual capabilities.
  • The company rolled out Wukong, an agentic AI platform, and expanded enterprise focused tools such as Accio Work.
  • Alibaba shifted its AI approach from open source releases to paid enterprise solutions and moved to an AI first hiring strategy, with AI roles now representing over 80% of open positions.

Alibaba Group Holding, trading at $122.05, is pushing deeper into AI at a time when the stock shows mixed longer term performance. NYSE:BABA is down 10.0% over the past 30 days and 21.6% year to date, while the 3 year return stands at 25.0% and the 5 year return shows a 43.7% decline. These moves into proprietary AI and agentic platforms arrive against that backdrop, giving investors fresh company specific developments to track.

For readers following NYSE:BABA, the shift toward paid AI offerings and heavy AI focused hiring could gradually reshape how the company generates and prices enterprise services. The combination of new models like Qwen3.5-Omni and Qwen3.6-Plus with platforms such as Wukong and Accio Work gives Alibaba several levers to pursue AI related revenue over time. How effectively the company converts this build out into customer adoption and monetization will be a key theme to watch.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Alibaba Group Holding by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Alibaba Group Holding.

4 things going right for Alibaba Group Holding that this headline doesn't cover.

Alibaba’s acceleration in proprietary AI models and agent-based platforms points to a clearer focus on enterprise-grade automation rather than just model benchmarks. Qwen3.5-Omni and Qwen3.6-Plus target real work use cases such as code generation, multimodal reasoning and complex task handling, while Wukong and Accio Work act as virtual employees for both large enterprises and small businesses. Combined with higher cloud and storage pricing and a move from open-source to paid access, Alibaba is effectively trying to turn its AI stack into a recurring revenue engine. The AI-first hiring shift, with AI roles now making up over 80% of open positions, underlines how much execution will depend on attracting and retaining top engineers in a crowded field that includes Tencent, Baidu and global players such as Microsoft and Amazon.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Alibaba Group Holding Narrative

  • The push into applied, agent-based AI directly supports the narrative that heavy AI and cloud spending is intended to build higher value enterprise services that can support future earnings quality.
  • Launching multiple proprietary models in quick succession, raising cloud prices and scaling AI hiring while recent earnings have come under pressure reinforces concerns in the narrative about margins staying under strain if monetization is slower than investment.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on AI and cloud capex and overall adoption, but it does not fully spell out how specific offerings like Wukong, Accio Work and Qwen3.6-Plus pricing tiers could influence contract structure and revenue mix.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Alibaba Group Holding to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk if enterprises and small businesses are slow to adopt paid Qwen models and Wukong-style tools at scale, which would leave Alibaba carrying higher AI platform and hiring costs against already pressured profitability.
  • ⚠️ Competitive pressure from Tencent, Baidu and US cloud providers that are also pushing multimodal, agent-based AI, which could limit pricing power or force higher spending on incentives to win and retain customers.
  • 🎁 A broader lineup of proprietary models and AI agents that can be tightly integrated with Alibaba Cloud, e commerce and payments, which may deepen customer stickiness and support more workflow-driven spending over time.
  • 🎁 The AI-first hiring strategy and move away from open-source giveaways give Alibaba greater control over product direction and monetization levers, which could help align future contracts more closely with usage and outcomes.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching whether Alibaba starts to share usage or revenue metrics tied specifically to Qwen models, Wukong and Accio Work, along with any data on enterprise renewals or seat expansion for these services. You may also want to track how often management links AI-driven offerings to its cloud and AI revenue ambitions, how AI-related capex trends versus net income, and whether competitor announcements from Tencent, Baidu or global cloud providers change the pace of product launches or pricing moves at Alibaba.

In the last twelve months, the biggest single purchase by an insider was when Chairman & MD Amrit Shah bought ₹67m worth of shares at a price of ₹15.00 per share. I like to look at how many shares insiders own in a company, to help inform my view of how aligned they are with insiders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. Moksh Ornaments insiders own 42% of the company, currently worth about ₹320m based on the recent share price. data.description:

Approach 1: Korn Ferry Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what Korn Ferry's future cash generation is worth in today's dollars.

For Korn Ferry, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is reported at about $305.3 million. Based on analyst inputs for the near term and Simply Wall St extrapolations after that, projected Free Cash Flow for 2035 is $394.2 million, with intermediate yearly projections between 2026 and 2034 ranging from $308.0 million to $381.6 million. These projections are all in $ and are modeled using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach.

Rolling all of those cash flows into the DCF model results in an estimated intrinsic value of $133.17 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $63.36, the model implies the stock is about 52.4% undervalued on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Korn Ferry.

Approach 2: Korn Ferry Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to connect what you pay per share with the earnings that support that price. It gives a quick sense of how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

Korn Ferry currently trades on a P/E of 12.39x. This sits below both the Professional Services industry average P/E of 18.98x and a peer group average of 17.23x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Korn Ferry of 18.87x, which reflects factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margin, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for these business characteristics rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. Comparing Korn Ferry’s current 12.39x P/E to the 18.87x Fair Ratio suggests the shares trade at a discount on this earnings based view.

Result: UNDERVALUED
